metric_name: Career start: PHI join date and salary
metric_value: Kastner joined PHI in March 1969 as a 21-year-old junior programmer earning $7,200/year.

metric_name: CPU time cost per hour
metric_value: $360 per hour on PHI's IBM System/360 Model 65 service bureau.

metric_name: Compute cost vs. labor cost equation (1969)
metric_value: 20 hours of machine time equaled one programmer's annual salary; machine was scarce, labor was cheap.

metric_name: Inversion of compute vs. labor scarcity
metric_value: By 2020s, compute is near-free and human talent is the scarce resource—the inverse of 1969.

metric_name: PHI headquarters description
metric_value: Converted funeral home in Arlington, Massachusetts; atmosphere half dormitory, half NASA mission control.

metric_name: Founder background: Apollo navigation code
metric_value: Philip Hankins wrote navigation code for the Apollo lunar program before founding PHI.

metric_name: Engineering lead background: Apollo navigation code
metric_value: David Moros co-wrote navigation code for Apollo and served as PHI engineering lead; described as dapper, polite, and relentlessly precise.

metric_name: Siegel's later career: Wang VS architect
metric_value: Robert A. Siegel later architected the Wang VS operating system after leaving PHI.

metric_name: Siegel's teaching philosophy: programmer manages physical resources
metric_value: An applications programmer manages physical machine resources: disk spin, core memory cycle, card reader click. Code is choreography of hardware.

metric_name: Learning tools available: reference card and legal pads
metric_value: No Stack Overflow, no Google, no online docs; only the IBM System/360 Reference Card (placemat-sized fold-out) and yellow legal pads.

metric_name: Learning hexadecimal core dump reading
metric_value: Kastner learned to read hexadecimal core dumps to find the 'ghost in the machine'—debugging equivalent of reconstructing a car crash from skid marks.

metric_name: Learning JCL (Job Control Language)
metric_value: Kastner learned JCL—the 'dark arts'—to control IBM OS job execution; single misplaced comma could fail a job and waste expensive machine time.

metric_name: Core dump scale
metric_value: Catastrophic program failure generated hundreds of pages of raw hexadecimal numbers representing entire core memory contents.

metric_name: JCL characteristics: cryptic and unforgiving
metric_value: JCL was notoriously cryptic; a single misplaced comma caused a job to fail and waste an expensive machine run.

metric_name: Siegel's One-Instruction Switch technique
metric_value: IBM instruction to set binary flag in register directly without touching memory; replaced 3 memory references with 1 instruction—transformative at scale.

metric_name: One-Instruction Switch achievable in COBOL
metric_value: Siegel confirmed the One-Instruction Switch optimization was achievable in COBOL, not just assembly.

metric_name: Economics of 1969 programming culture
metric_value: Efficiency was not an engineering preference; it was economic survival at $360/hour. Unnecessary instructions were a small theft from the company budget.

metric_name: Service bureau batch submission workflow
metric_value: Write code on coding form → keypunch to cards → submit to operations queue → wait hours → retrieve printout → iterate on error.
source_page: Why $360 an Hour Changed How Software Was Written sidebar

metric_name: Timesharing availability: universities vs. commercial bureaus
metric_value: Timesharing (terminal-based interactive coding) existed at universities and research labs in 1969 but was not typical at commercial service bureaus.
source_page: Why $360 an Hour Changed How Software Was Written sidebar

metric_name: Batch submission imposed rigorous pre-coding discipline
metric_value: Every submission was a financial event; discipline of thinking through problems completely before submission was rigorous in ways instant-feedback IDEs don't replicate.
source_page: Why $360 an Hour Changed How Software Was Written sidebar

metric_name: Cumberland Farms pump counter rollover problem
metric_value: Pump counters physically rolled over to zero at 9,999 gallons; accounting code had to detect rollover and reconcile digital ledger against paper cash transmittal forms.

metric_name: Lesson: software models physical reality, not ideal specifications
metric_value: Good software anticipates the ways reality deviates from specification; models what the pump's gears actually did, not what the pump should have done.

metric_name: Marine Midland bond portfolio Y2K fix date
metric_value: July 1969; while Apollo 11 transfixed the world, Kastner was fixing a Y2K-class two-digit-year bug in Marine Midland Bank's bond portfolio system.

metric_name: Y2K bug discovered 30 years early
metric_value: Two-digit year field: 30-year bond issued in 1970 maturing in 2000 would be recorded as '00', read as 1900. Kastner expanded field to fix it.

metric_name: Y2K fix made quietly, no press release
metric_value: "No one wrote a press release about it. We were just doing the job." Fix came 30 years before the global Y2K panic.

metric_name: Cosmos system description and naming
metric_value: Originally called Customer Accounting System (CAS), renamed Cosmos; Creedence Clearwater Revival 'Cosmos Factory' album cover framed on main room wall.

metric_name: Cosmos capability: code generation at scale
metric_value: Cosmos generated millions of lines of architecturally sound COBOL code according to specifications—a generator, not a conventional application.

metric_name: Code generation as answer to Brooks's Law
metric_value: Code generator sidesteps Brooks's Law coordination overhead; produces code faster than any team with perfect architectural consistency—a force multiplier.

metric_name: Code generation as early low-code development
metric_value: Cosmos was an early form of low-code development, enabling PHI to deploy systems at scale and speed impossible with conventional programming teams.

metric_name: Cosmos productivity: bank checking account system build time
metric_value: Team of four built an entire bank checking account system using Cosmos in one year.

metric_name: Personal excitement working on Cosmos
metric_value: Working on Cosmos was 'genuinely exciting—the closest I had come so far to the automation dream'—building a machine that built other machines.

metric_name: Wang acquires PHI and relocates staff
metric_value: Wang Laboratories acquired PHI; professional staff ordered to relocate to Wang's facility in Tewksbury, Massachusetts (~25 miles north of Boston).

metric_name: Reason for leaving PHI: commute, not dissatisfaction
metric_value: Kastner's wife worked in Quincy (South Shore); Tewksbury (North Shore) commute through Boston was 'Commute of Death'—impractical daily commitment.

metric_name: Wang described as remarkable company
metric_value: Kastner had nothing against Wang Laboratories; called it 'a remarkable company'—and noted Moros provided key technical leadership for An Wang's computing initiatives.

metric_name: Moros role at Wang post-acquisition
metric_value: David Moros provided key technical leadership for Dr. An Wang's computing initiatives after PHI's acquisition by Wang Laboratories.

metric_name: PHI diaspora: Murray Sherry leads group to Arthur D. Little Systems
metric_value: Kastner followed Murray Sherry and others from PHI to Arthur D. Little Systems in Cambridge; described as 'a small, seasoned rescue squad.'

metric_name: PHI alumni group joined Arthur D. Little Systems
metric_value: Several PHI alumni arrived together at Arthur D. Little Systems, Cambridge—described as 'arriving as a small, seasoned rescue squad' rather than joining a consultancy.

metric_name: Lesson 1: software models physical reality
metric_value: Gas pump counters roll over; bank systems use two-digit years. Good code anticipates the gap between specification and reality. Application users matter.

metric_name: Lesson 2: economics shape engineering culture more than aesthetics
metric_value: PHI programmers' obsession with instruction counts was rational response to $360/hour, not personality. Change the economics, change the culture.

metric_name: Lesson 3: automation multiplied by systems beats automation multiplied by people
metric_value: Cosmos showed the right answer to Brooks's Law was tools that made each programmer more powerful, not more programmers.

metric_name: AI inference cost trajectory analogy to 1969 compute costs
metric_value: Falling AI inference costs in late 2020s will re-invert the economics again—echoing the 1969 dynamic in reverse. Worth remembering.

metric_name: Kastner's starting age and compensation
metric_value: Age 21, salary $7,200/year—described as higher than what many bank vice presidents earned at the time.

metric_name: CPU cost relative to annual salary
metric_value: 20 hours of IBM 360/65 CPU time at $360/hour = $7,200 = one programmer's annual salary in 1969.

metric_name: PHI staff composition
metric_value: Collection of whiz kids and productive eccentrics spread across a ramshackle group of old houses and a repurposed funeral home in Arlington, MA.

metric_name: PHI sloppy code culture
metric_value: At PHI, sloppy code was not merely an error—it was a moral failing. Apollo-derived DNA ran through everything they built.

metric_name: Siegel character description
metric_value: Brilliant, abrasive mentor. Did not teach syntax; taught physics of the machine—physical resources of disk, memory, card reader.

metric_name: Moros character description
metric_value: Described as 'dapper, polite, and relentlessly precise'; presided over PHI's engineering team.

metric_name: Core dump debugging analogy
metric_value: Reading a core dump was like reconstructing a car crash from skid marks—hundreds of pages of raw hex to locate program crash cause and data state.

metric_name: Cumberland Farms project type
metric_value: Retail accounting system for New England gas station and convenience store chain; one of Kastner's first PHI assignments.

metric_name: Software is not mathematics
metric_value: Software is a model of a much messier physical world; the Cumberland Farms and Marine Midland projects drove this lesson home.

metric_name: Bond portfolio system context: Apollo 11 concurrent
metric_value: Kastner was fixing the Y2K bug in July 1969 while the rest of the world was watching Apollo 11.

metric_name: Y2K root cause: two-digit year fields
metric_value: Systems with two-digit year fields stored year 2000 as '00', interpreted as 1900—a design choice consistent with 1960s storage economics.

metric_name: Fix method: expand maturity date field
metric_value: Kastner fixed the Marine Midland Y2K bug by expanding the maturity date field to accommodate four-digit years.

metric_name: Cosmos vs. conventional COBOL teams
metric_value: In 1969, most large apps were built by teams writing COBOL by hand one procedure at a time; Cosmos automated this with perfect architectural consistency.

metric_name: Brooks's Law and its relevance to code generation
metric_value: Frederick Brooks argued adding programmers to a late project makes it later; coordination cost grows faster than productivity added. Code generation sidesteps this.
notes: Reference to The Mythical Man-Month by Frederick Brooks.

metric_name: Cosmos enabled scale impossible for conventional teams
metric_value: Cosmos allowed PHI to deploy systems at a scale and speed impossible with conventional programming teams; a force multiplier.

metric_name: Cosmos connected to childhood automation dream
metric_value: Cosmos felt like the closest realization of Kastner's childhood automation dream since working in a restaurant bookkeeping room.

metric_name: Tewksbury facility location relative to Boston
metric_value: Wang's Tewksbury facility approximately 25 miles north of Boston; on opposite side of city from South Shore employment center.

metric_name: Decision framework: left PHI due to commute, not work
metric_value: 'It was not the machine that drove me out of PHI. It was the Massachusetts Turnpike and Route 128 at 7:30 in the morning.'

metric_name: PHI years duration
metric_value: 1969–1973 at PHI: constituted an education no business school offered and no curriculum could have designed.

metric_name: Transition framing: physics of machine to physics of organizations
metric_value: 'The physics of the machine were now in my bones. It was time to learn the physics of organizations.'

metric_name: Batch submission process: financial implications
metric_value: Each card deck submission was a financial event; careless programs failing on first run wasted real money, not just time.
source_page: Why $360 an Hour Changed How Software Was Written sidebar

metric_name: Core memory as scarce resource
metric_value: Programmers had to manage core memory cycles explicitly; efficient use of memory was economically critical at $360/hour.

metric_name: PHI as service bureau business model
metric_value: PHI operated IBM 360/65 as a service bureau, selling CPU time at $360/hour to client companies.

metric_name: Kastner's junior programmer role at PHI
metric_value: Joined as 21-year-old junior programmer; received education no business school offered through hands-on work with machine economics and senior mentors.

metric_name: Marine Midland engagement type
metric_value: PHI client engagement for bond portfolio system upgrade in July 1969.

metric_name: Apollo program culture transplanted to PHI
metric_value: PHI's zero-defect culture derived from Apollo: for Apollo programmers, a bug didn't mean a system crash—it meant a spacecraft missing the moon.

metric_name: Philip Hankins role at PHI
metric_value: Founder of PHI; Apollo veteran whose engineering DNA shaped the company culture.

metric_name: An Wang's computing initiatives post-PHI acquisition
metric_value: David Moros provided key technical leadership for Dr. An Wang's computing initiatives following Wang Labs' acquisition of PHI.

metric_name: COBOL as dominant commercial language at service bureaus
metric_value: COBOL was the primary language for large application systems at commercial service bureaus in 1969; teams wrote it by hand one procedure at a time.

metric_name: IBM 360 as dominant platform for service bureau computing
metric_value: IBM System/360 family was the dominant platform at commercial service bureaus in late 1960s; reference card was primary programmer documentation.

metric_name: JCL mastery as differentiator
metric_value: Mastering JCL separated people who understood the system from those who merely used it—not glamorous but critical knowledge.

metric_name: Siegel's efficiency culture philosophy
metric_value: Efficiency culture at PHI was direct product of $360/hour economics: when machine time is scarce, programmer's job is to minimize consumption of it.
source_page: Why $360 an Hour Changed How Software Was Written sidebar

metric_name: Programmer as resource manager, not mathematician
metric_value: Kastner internalized from Siegel that a programmer's job is choreographing hardware, not solving mathematical problems.

metric_name: Cross-reference: technology breadth analysis
metric_value: See companion study kastner-technology-breadth-memoir-2026 for quantified 47-year coverage breadth (2,537 distinct technologies, 14 thematic rollups).
